result.reasoning: Verified against actual code. pyproject.toml lines 1-3,7 declare build-system.requires=["hatchling"], build-backend="hatchling.build", and dynamic=["version"], so an editable install triggers PEP 517 build isolation that fetches hatchling + transitive build deps (editables, trove-classifiers) live from PyPI. All three CI jobs (.github/workflows/ci.yml lines 35, 80, 191) use `pip install --no-deps -e .`; `--no-deps` suppresses runtime deps but does NOT disable build isolation — only `--no-build-isolation` would. grep confirms zero hatchling entries in requirements.lock and requirements-core.lock, and there is no `--no-build-isolation` flag or separate hashed-wheel build anywhere in CI. Therefore the build toolchain is fetched un-pinned, un-hashed, and unseen by pip-audit (which scans only the lockfiles) and the SBOM. This directly contradicts the workflow's own comments claiming installs use 'EXACTLY the versions pip-audit scanned' and that the #F-004 arbitrary-code-execution window is closed: a compromised hatchling/editables release would execute arbitrary code during the editable build on every run, undetected. Reproducibility is also broken (yanked/changed build backend => non-deterministic green). Not by-design and not speculative — it is a concrete gap in the project's stated hash-pin/reproducibility guarantee. Severity high rather than critical because it affects the CI build toolchain, not the shipped runtime artifact, and exploitation requires an upstream PyPI compromise of a maintained package.

Confirmed in current code. ci.yml installs the editable package with `pip install --no-deps -e .` at lines 35 (core), 80 (integration), and 191 (audit/license-gate). `--no-deps` suppresses runtime deps but does NOT disable PEP 517 build isolation, and none of the three add `--no-build-isolation` (grep: 0 matches). pyproject.toml lines 1-3 declare build-system.requires=["hatchling"] / build-backend="hatchling.build", and project.version is `dynamic` resolved via [tool.hatch.version] (lines 7, 76-77). Therefore every editable install creates an isolated build env and fetches hatchling + its deps (editables, trove-classifiers, pathspec, packaging) live from PyPI, un-pinned and un-hashed, executing hatchling build-hook code under the test/audit context. Neither lockfile carries the build toolchain: `grep -ic 'hatchling|editables|trove-classifiers' requirements.lock requirements-core.lock` returns 0 for both. pip-audit and the SBOM only scan `-r requirements.lock`, so the build-time dependency set is never audited and absent from the SBOM, and --require-hashes does not cover the isolated build env. This directly contradicts the workflow's own comments asserting it installs "EXACTLY the versions pip-audit scanned" and that the #F-004 arbitrary-code-execution window is closed — the window remains open for the build backend. Reproducible and worth fixing; the proposed fix is correct. Rated high rather than critical because runtime/test deps ARE hash-pinned, so the exposure is limited to a single well-known backend's build-time dependency set.

Confirmed against actual code at scripts/capture_transcripts.py:108-113. _copy_scrubbed() does `try: text = raw.decode("utf-8") except UnicodeDecodeError: data = raw` (else: scrub). A single invalid UTF-8 byte anywhere in a transcript raises UnicodeDecodeError and routes the ENTIRE file to the verbatim branch, copied byte-for-byte with no redaction. Reproduced the PoC: input `b'{"out":"[REDACTED] caf\xe9byte=\x80 /Users/[REDACTED]/x"}'` -> branch=verbatim, [REDACTED] token LEAKED, operator username '[REDACTED]' LEAKED, no REDACTED present. With errors='replace' the identical content is fully scrubbed (token redacted, username redacted, REDACTED present). This is a real regression: the comment at lines 104-106 documents that CV-O-1 broadened scrubbing to all files because the old gate copied .json RAW; but the new strict-decode-then-verbatim classifier reintroduces a fail-open path precisely for the files most likely to embed raw subprocess stdout (hexdumps, latin-1/non-UTF-8 file content, truncated multibyte) inside JSONL string values — exactly the content carrying dumped secrets/PII. UTF-8 invalidity != binary, so the classification is wrong. It silently defeats the load-bearing privacy guarantee (no credential-shaped secrets, no operator email/username/path) for affected committed provenance files. Fix is sound and low-risk: decode with errors='replace' for scrubbing, and gate any verbatim path on a real binary signal (b'\x00' in raw) instead of UTF-8 validity; add a regression test for a .jsonl containing both a secret and an invalid byte. Not by-design and not speculative — reproducible. Severity high rather than critical because the trigger requires an invalid byte to be present and the README already cautions that transcripts may contain PII and should be reviewed before any public release; the broken guarantee is the defense-in-depth redaction layer.

Confirmed in current code at scripts/capture_transcripts.py:108-113. _copy_scrubbed() decodes raw bytes with strict UTF-8: `try: text = raw.decode('utf-8') except UnicodeDecodeError: data = raw`. On any UnicodeDecodeError the entire file is assigned to the verbatim branch (data = raw) and written byte-for-byte with zero scrubbing. The else branch (scrubbing via _scrub) only runs when decode fully succeeds. Reproduced the PoC: a JSONL value containing `[REDACTED]...`, `/Users/[REDACTED]/x`, plus one invalid byte (0xe9) raises UnicodeDecodeError, so the file is copied verbatim with `[REDACTED]` present (True), `[REDACTED]` present (True), and no `REDACTED` (False). The `errors='replace'` decode succeeds and lets _scrub run normally. The outer `except Exception` at line 123 does not catch this because the decode-error is handled internally and execution proceeds to write raw bytes. The defect is real because: (1) it is the new CV-O-1 code that introduced fail-open behavior (per finding, the pre-CV-O-1 .jsonl path used errors='replace' and always scrubbed); (2) the verbatim path is gated on UTF-8 validity rather than a real binary signal (NUL byte), and UTF-8-invalid != binary; (3) Claude tool-result transcripts commonly embed arbitrary subprocess stdout (hexdumps, latin-1 reads, truncated multibyte) inside JSONL strings, making invalid bytes a realistic, common condition; and (4) it silently disables the system's primary privacy guarantee for precisely the files most likely to carry raw sensitive data. Fix is sound: decode with errors='replace' before scrubbing, and if a binary verbatim path is still desired, gate it on `b'\x00' in raw` rather than UTF-8 validity; add a regression test (jsonl with a secret + an invalid byte must come out scrubbed).

Reproduced against current code at /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay. scripts/capture_transcripts.py:38-91 redacts the operator email, the bare home-dir username ('[REDACTED]'), and the username segment in paths/slugs, but never the operator's real full display name. git log -1 --format=%an returns '[REDACTED]', confirming this is the operator identity. grep -rc '[REDACTED]' transcripts/ returns 252 committed files containing it in cleartext. The name appears directly beside successfully-redacted fields in committed transcripts (e.g. pyproject `{ name = "[REDACTED]", email = "[REDACTED]" }`, GitHub issue `author: thehipsterciso ([REDACTED])`, and cwd=/Users/[REDACTED]/...), so the masking of email/username is undercut by the adjacent cleartext name. No test in tests/ covers display-name redaction (0 matches for '[REDACTED]'/user.name/SCRUB_NAMES), confirming an unintentional gap rather than a documented scope decision. The leak is real, reproducible, and contradicts the stated purpose of the #F-024/#G-007 de-identification work. The suggested fix is sound: derive the display name from `git config user.name` and/or a configurable env list, escape + word-boundary + length-guard it (mirroring _username_pattern), apply in _scrub, add a regression test, and re-capture/scrub history to remediate the 252 already-committed files. Severity is bounded below critical/high: the README documents the transcripts-contain-PII caveat and instructs review before publishing, the wheel never ships transcripts, and exposure requires publishing the repo. But it is a genuine, code-level de-identification hole leaking distinct operator PII into committed history, warranting a fix — medium.

Reproduced all core claims in current code at /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay. (1) `git log -1 --format='%an'` returns '[REDACTED]' — the operator identity. (2) `grep -rc '[REDACTED]' transcripts/` returns 252 files with cleartext occurrences. (3) Committed transcripts contain the full name directly beside a successfully-redacted email, e.g. `Author: [REDACTED] <[REDACTED]>` and `Co-authored-by: [REDACTED] <[REDACTED]>`. (4) Running the real `_scrub()` on `Author: [REDACTED] <[REDACTED]>` yields `Author: [REDACTED] <[REDACTED]>` — email masked, full display name leaked in cleartext.

scripts/capture_transcripts.py:38-91 deliberately redacts operator PII: email (line 50), home-dir username in paths (51), the project-dir slug username (55), and the bare username token via _username_pattern() (59-90). The operator's full DISPLAY name is distinct, equivalent-tier PII and is NOT in the redaction set, so it leaks via git author/co-author strings and prose. tests/test_scripts.py has zero coverage for full-name redaction, confirming an unintentional gap rather than a documented by-design scope decision — the module docstring and #F-024/#G-007 work establish that de-identifying operator identity (so committed provenance does not publish it) IS the intent, which leaving the adjacent full name defeats.

One inaccuracy in the PoC: its literal example `_scrub('Author: [REDACTED] <a@b.c>')` does NOT mask the email either, because `.c` is a 1-char TLD that fails the `[A-Za-z]{2,}` email pattern; with a realistic email the described behavior (email masked, name leaked) holds exactly. Also pyproject.toml authors uses 'thehipsterciso', not the real name, so that specific listed vector is absent — but the git-author and prose vectors are real and pervasive (252 files). These do not undermine the core finding.

Severity medium, consistent with how the prior email/username leaks (#F-024/#G-007) were treated: this is operator-PII leakage already committed to a repo whose own docstring/README frame it for public-release review, not an exploitable code-execution flaw. The fix is straightforward (derive name from `git config user.name` / a configurable ASSAY_SCRUB_NAMES list, re.escape + word-boundary + length-guard, mirror _username_pattern, add a test), and remediating committed files requires re-capture or history scrub.

Confirmed in current code. tracking.py:64-68 is a genuine check-then-create TOCTOU: _experiment_id() calls get_experiment_by_name() and, if None, create_experiment() with no race handling. Two concurrent first-time start_run() calls for a brand-new experiment name can both see None and both call create_experiment(); MLflow rejects the loser with RESOURCE_ALREADY_EXISTS, which propagates out of _experiment_id -> start_run (note: it propagates before create_run, so unlike the #F-023 path no orphan run is even created here — the experiment-creation step is entirely unguarded). The concurrency preconditions hold: an 8-worker ThreadPoolExecutor exists (reasoning/seam.py:191-192) and multiple study processes sharing one local SQLite store is the documented model. Consumer impact is exactly as described: pipeline.py:351-373 catches the start_run exception, leaves run_id=None (commented inline at line 361), after which the track() helper no-ops on run_id is None (line 319), so all metrics, the run_duration_s SLO metric (line 727), and the provenance-trail artifact correlation (lines 731+) are silently dropped for that study. No test covers the duplicate-create path — test_observability.py:139-144 only exercises create_experiment succeeding. The proposed fix (try/except around create_experiment, re-fetch via get_experiment_by_name on duplicate-name error, mirroring mlflow.set_experiment's get-or-create idiom) is correct and minimal. Severity medium rather than high: the race window is one-time-per-experiment-name (once created, the get_experiment_by_name branch always wins), and the impact is observability data for a single study, which the module already treats as strictly best-effort (the run itself never aborts). Real, reproducible, worth fixing, but bounded window and non-correctness impact.

Confirmed against actual code at /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/.github/workflows/ci.yml:183-194 and scripts/license_gate.py.

The license-gate step comment (ci.yml:186-189) claims it scans "the SAME hash-pinned set pip-audit audited." The actual command (ci.yml:193) is `pip-licenses --format=json --with-system`, which enumerates EVERY distribution in site-packages, not requirements.lock. By line 193 the env contains: the lock set (line 190), the editable project `-e .` (line 191), pip-licenses==5.5.5 and its transitive deps prettytable/wcwidth (line 192), plus pip/setuptools/wheel pulled in by --with-system. license_gate.py (lines 34-47) iterates the entire JSON with no filter to requirements.lock package names, so it genuinely checks this superset. The PoC is accurate: the scanned set != the lock set.

Pinning-inconsistency sub-claim is also accurate: line 192 (`python -m pip install pip-licenses==5.5.5`) omits --require-hashes, whereas the lock install (line 190) and pip-audit (line 153) both use --require-hashes.

Severity is low rather than high/critical: the over-inclusion makes the gate strictly MORE conservative (it would also flag copyleft in tooling), so it cannot let a copyleft production dep slip through — it can only cause a spurious failure if a tooling dep ever ships copyleft. The current tool deps (pip-licenses MIT, prettytable BSD, wcwidth MIT, pip/setuptools/wheel MIT/Apache) do not trip the _DENY list, so there is no current CI failure. The real problem is (a) the comment is materially false, misleading future maintainers about what the production license verdict covers, and (b) coupling the Apache-2.0-distribution license attestation to dev/tooling packages is a latent false-positive source and a correctness/design defect. Worth fixing (drop --with-system or drive from lock package names; install pip-licenses in an isolated step; add --require-hashes), but not a security-blocking production defect.

Confirmed at the code level. tracking.py:64-68 (_experiment_id) does get_experiment_by_name() then unconditionally create_experiment() if None — a real check-then-create TOCTOU with no try/except for the duplicate-name case. The downstream consequence is accurate and confirmed in the consuming code: pipeline.py:351-370 catches a start_run exception, leaves run_id=None, and the track() helper (pipeline.py:317-320) then no-ops every metric/SLO/artifact call for the whole study. No test exercises the already-exists fallback (tests/test_observability.py only covers create_experiment succeeding and a later log_param failure).

Two corrections to the finding's reachability claim: (1) The cited "8-thread reasoning pool" (seam.py:191-192) is NOT a path to start_run — those workers call the LLM client, not the tracker — so the in-process race within a single run_study is not real via that mechanism. (2) The genuine, reproducible trigger is the multi-process angle, which is explicitly by design: the module docstring/ADR-0003 describe a local on-box SQLite store, and multiple study processes sharing it is intended. The race is real only on the FIRST use of each new experiment name (once it exists, get_experiment_by_name returns it for all callers), when two processes start near-simultaneously; one loses with RESOURCE_ALREADY_EXISTS.

Real and worth fixing — the proposed get-or-create fallback mirrors mlflow.set_experiment and is low-risk. Severity is low rather than higher because: the window is narrow (first use per experiment name only), it requires concurrent process startup, and the failure mode is already partially mitigated (the run degrades loudly to best-effort no-op with a logged warning and trail record rather than crashing or corrupting data).

Verified against current code at /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay.

CI workflow (.github/workflows/ci.yml lines 183-194): the License gate step's comment (lines 187-189) explicitly claims it scans "the SAME hash-pinned set pip-audit audited." The actual command on line 193 is `pip-licenses --format=json --with-system`, which enumerates EVERY distribution in site-packages, not just requirements.lock. scripts/license_gate.py confirms there is no filtering: main() iterates over every package object in the JSON (line 34) and applies the deny scan to all of them. So the license-verified set is a strict superset of requirements.lock.

By the time line 193 runs, site-packages additionally contains: pip-licenses==5.5.5 and its transitive deps (prettytable, wcwidth) installed on line 192, plus pip/setuptools/wheel surfaced by --with-system, plus the editable project install from line 191. The comment's assertion is therefore factually false, and the production-distribution license verdict is coupled to dev/tooling/system packages — a copyleft license appearing in any future tool dep would spuriously fail the gate (e.g. via the GPL/AGPL/SSPL/EUPL substrings in _DENY).

Pinning inconsistency also confirmed: line 192 installs pip-licenses==5.5.5 without --require-hashes, unlike the locked install on line 190. (Caveat: line 191 `pip install --no-deps -e .` is likewise not hash-pinned, so the finding's "every other install in the job" framing is slightly overstated, but the inconsistency itself is real.)

This is real and reproducible (the scanned package set demonstrably != requirements.lock), not speculative or by-design — the comment proves intent diverged from implementation. It is worth fixing for correctness and to decouple the gate from tooling.

Severity is low, not high: today none of the extra packages (pip-licenses MIT, prettytable BSD, wcwidth MIT, pip/setuptools/wheel MIT) carry a denied license, so the gate does not actually misfire in the current state; the impact is a latent correctness/robustness gap plus a misleading comment, with no active CI failure or security exposure.

The code claim is factually accurate: in /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/scripts/capture_transcripts.py, _copy_scrubbed scrubs file CONTENT (line 113) but returns the raw relative path string (line 119), and main() serializes MANIFEST.json (lines 153-166) with active_session=sid (line 157, =session.stem) and each rec['path'] without calling _scrub(). So the manifest's string fields do bypass the redaction path. This matches the structural class of the already-fixed CV-O-1.

However, this is NOT a true production-readiness problem in current code — it is speculative/defense-in-depth, not a reproducible defect:
- I verified the actual source filenames in ~/.claude/projects/-Users-[REDACTED]-hc-grc/: every top-level session is an opaque UUID (e.g. 8d54714a-d616-4911-a386-9b4b0f2ce74d) and every subtree file is an agent-id hash (agent-a05067d59cf434caf) or .meta.json. The committed transcripts/MANIFEST.json confirms this: active_session is a UUID and all 2479 path entries are UUID/agent-hash stems. Zero PII present.
- The PII that the redaction system actually targets (home-dir username, emails, project-dir slug) lives in file CONTENT, which IS scrubbed. The Claude transcript naming scheme does not embed operator-identifying content in filenames.
- The leak requires a hypothetical future change where some transcript is named with a username/slug/path — which is not how the Claude project directory names files. The finding itself rates it LOW and frames it as "defense-in-depth that closes the structural gap for future filename shapes."

Real and accurately described, but no reproducible exposure exists in current code; this is a speculative hardening nicety, so it does not meet the "real, reproducible, worth fixing (not speculative)" bar for confirmation. The suggested fix (scrub the json blob or the string fields) is cheap and harmless, but it addresses a non-existent current leak.

The code description is factually accurate: in scripts/capture_transcripts.py, MANIFEST.json is written via json.dumps() (lines 153-166) and the dict's string fields bypass _scrub() — each rec['path'] is str(dstf.relative_to(...)) at line 119, and active_session is sid = session.stem (lines 134, 157). File CONTENT is scrubbed in _copy_scrubbed (line 113) but the manifest path strings are not. That part is verified.

However, this does not meet the bar for a confirmed production-readiness problem. It is not reproducible in current code: the manifest's path/active_session fields are derived entirely from Claude session filenames, which are opaque UUID/agent-id stems (sid = session.stem is a session UUID; subtree entries are agent/workflow names). These filenames are not operator-controlled and never embed username/home-path/slug/email content, so no PII reaches the manifest today. The finding itself concedes "nothing leaks in practice — hence LOW" and the leak is entirely conditional on a hypothetical future ("If any future transcript file... is ever named with operator-identifying content").

The actual redaction patterns (_SECRET_PATTERNS, _USERNAME_RE) target the content shapes that do leak (emails, /Users/[REDACTED] slugs, bare username, credential tokens) — none of which appear in Claude session filenames. So this is a speculative defense-in-depth hardening item against a filename shape that does not occur, not a real reproducible leak. The script also exits 0 on all errors and is provenance-only (transcripts/ are never packaged in the wheel per the module docstring), further bounding impact. The proposed fix is cheap and harmless, but the bar here excludes speculative/by-design items, and this is speculative.

Verified against actual code at /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay.

Class hierarchy (src/assay_engine/reasoning/seam.py:66-75): both PermanentReasoningError (line 70) and RateLimitError (line 74) subclass ReasoningError (line 66). So pytest.raises(ReasoningError) catches all three types.

Retry executor branches on EXACT subtype (seam.py:597-610): `except PermanentReasoningError: raise` (no retry), `except RateLimitError:` (long RATE_LIMIT_BACKOFF backpressure, capped at RATE_LIMIT_MAX_RETRIES), `except ReasoningError:` (fast BACKOFF_BASE**transient exponential, capped at MAX_RETRIES). The transient-vs-permanent-vs-ratelimit distinction therefore has real, divergent runtime consequences (retry/no-retry and backoff strategy).

The three flagged tests assert only the base class:
- test_t3_empty_reply_raises_transient (line 145): pytest.raises(ReasoningError)
- test_t3_generic_error_maps_to_transient (a 500, line 170): pytest.raises(ReasoningError)
- test_t3_sdk_raises_mid_stream_maps_to_transient (line 205): pytest.raises(ReasoningError)

Because pytest.raises catches subclasses, a misclassification (e.g. mapping a transient 500 to PermanentReasoningError -> not retried, or to RateLimitError -> wrong backoff) still passes. The PoC is sound: replacing the generic fall-through with PermanentReasoningError would keep these tests green while silently making a retryable 500 non-retryable. The same file's sibling tests correctly pin exact subtypes (auth 401/403 -> PermanentReasoningError lines 164/190; 529 overloaded -> RateLimitError line 184; rejected -> RateLimitError lines 152/178/198), confirming this is an isolated non-discriminating gap rather than an intentional convention.

This is a real, reproducible test-quality defect worth fixing: the tests claim to guard the transient classification but do not, so a transient->permanent/rate-limit regression would ship undetected. It is not speculative or by-design. Severity medium rather than high because current production classification code is correct; this is latent regression risk in the test suite, not an active runtime fault. Recommended fix as described: capture the exception and assert `type(ei.value) is ReasoningError` and `not isinstance(ei.value, (PermanentReasoningError, RateLimitError))` for all three tests (or introduce a dedicated TransientReasoningError type).

Confirmed against current code. In src/assay_engine/reasoning/seam.py both PermanentReasoningError (line 70) and RateLimitError (line 74) subclass ReasoningError (line 66). The retry executor branches on exact subtype: PermanentReasoningError => re-raise/no retry (line 599-600), RateLimitError => long backpressure RATE_LIMIT_BACKOFF (601-605), base ReasoningError => fast BACKOFF_BASE backoff (606-609). So retry behavior depends entirely on which subtype the classifier raises.

The three transient tests in tests/integration/test_reasoning_drain.py — test_t3_empty_reply_raises_transient (145), test_t3_generic_error_maps_to_transient (170), test_t3_sdk_raises_mid_stream_maps_to_transient (205) — assert only pytest.raises(ReasoningError), the base class. Since pytest.raises catches subclasses, these pass even if the code misclassifies a transient error as PermanentReasoningError or RateLimitError. The PoC is reproducible: changing line 498 (raise ReasoningError ... status=500) to PermanentReasoningError makes a transient 500 non-retryable, yet test_t3_generic_error_maps_to_transient still passes. The transient-vs-permanent distinction the tests claim to guard is exactly what is not asserted.

This is an isolated weakness, not a convention: sibling tests correctly pin exact subtypes (test_t3_auth_error_maps_to_permanent:164 -> PermanentReasoningError; test_t3_overloaded_529_maps_to_ratelimit:184 -> RateLimitError; test_t3_rate_limit_event_rejected_maps_to_ratelimit:151).

Severity medium rather than high: no current production misbehavior — it is a test-quality/regression-detection gap. But it leaves the retry-classification logic (which determines whether transient frontier errors are retried or permanently dropped) unguarded against a real, plausible regression. Fix is the proposed discriminating assertion (capture exc and assert type is exactly ReasoningError and not isinstance of the subtypes) or a dedicated TransientReasoningError. Worth fixing.
